practices.statement: Keep repository copilot-instructions.md as the durable baseline and add extra layers (agents, skills, prompts) only when a repeated workflow clearly justifies the complexity.
Prefer references to existing docs, tasks, or files over duplicating the same guidance across prompts, instructions, and skills. Single source of truth prevents drift.
Validate frontmatter fields, file placement, and tool names before judging content quality. Broken metadata silently defeats good guidance — a prompt missing '---' fences is just ignored markdown.

Use the Chat Customizations editor (⇧⌘P → 'Chat: Open Chat Customizations') to discover, create, and manage all customizations from one place. It shows what's loaded, what's broken, and what's available.

Place workspace instructions in .github/instructions/, agents in .github/agents/, skills in .github/skills/, prompts in .github/prompts/, hooks in .github/hooks/. User-level variants go in ~/.copilot/ equivalents.
Use applyTo globs in .instructions.md frontmatter to scope instructions to specific file types or directories. This keeps language-specific rules from polluting other file edits.

When building multi-agent workflows, design handoff chains with clear transition prompts. Each handoff should specify what context to send ('conversation' for full context, 'summary' for compressed) and what the next agent needs to know.

Include build commands and test commands in copilot-instructions.md so agents can verify their work. Production agents like desktop/desktop's deskocat explicitly call build verification commands before delivering.

Use skills for progressive loading — the model sees only skill names and descriptions during routing, then reads the full SKILL.md only when invoked. This saves context budget for actually relevant methodology.

Use hooks to run formatters and linters automatically after agent file edits. This is more reliable than instructing the agent to format — hooks guarantee execution regardless of how the agent was prompted.
Use the /create-agent, /create-skill, /create-prompt, and /create-hook AI generation commands to bootstrap new customization files. They ask clarifying questions and generate correct frontmatter.

When a workflow spans multiple workspaces or users, use a shared cache or community repository as a bootstrap source, then verify and adapt conclusions locally. Never blindly paste community patterns without revalidation.
Use Claude-compatible file locations (.claude/agents/, .claude/skills/, .claude/settings.json) alongside VS Code locations for cross-tool compatibility. VS Code reads Claude format natively.

Use risk classification in agent workflows. Production agents like desktop/desktop categorize changes into low/medium/high risk and adjust verification requirements accordingly (low: lint, medium: tests, high: full review).
Use argument-hint in skill and prompt frontmatter to guide users on what input to provide. This appears as placeholder text in the chat input and significantly improves user experience.

For security-sensitive operations, use PreToolUse hooks to block dangerous commands (rm -rf, DROP TABLE, force push) before they execute. This provides a deterministic safety net that instructions alone cannot guarantee.
Use SessionStart hooks to inject project context (version, branch, environment) into every agent session. This is more reliable than relying on the agent to discover project state.
Use Given-When-Then acceptance criteria in agent instructions to define success conditions. This format is precise, testable, and resistant to ambiguous interpretation by the model.
Import shared skills from community repositories (github/awesome-copilot, anthropics/skills) using git submodules or direct file copy. Skills are designed to be portable across workspaces and tools.
When testing customization changes, use the Agent Debug Panel (View → Agent Debug Panel) to see which instructions, skills, and tools are loaded. This is more reliable than guessing from behavior.

When auditing technology-specific instruction files, assess content on three dimensions: (1) syntactic validity — does it load and apply correctly; (2) content depth — does it address design philosophy, architectural tradeoffs, and judgment calls that genuinely differ from AI defaults for this domain; (3) signal-to-noise — is it focused on non-obvious guidance, or is it padded with rules the toolchain already enforces.

Use the community cache's domain-knowledge entries as the assessment baseline for technology-specific instruction files. If the cache has principles or practices for the relevant domain, cross-reference them against the actual instruction file content before rating quality. An instruction file whose content falls well below the domain baseline should be flagged for enrichment.

Classify instruction file content quality in four explicit tiers: (1) broken — structural or metadata problems preventing the file from loading; (2) thin — structurally valid but only restating linter-level defaults or what the AI already knows; (3) adequate — covers key domain judgment calls without depth or tradeoff awareness; (4) excellent — addresses design philosophy, architectural tradeoffs, testability principles, and canonical judgment calls for the domain. Report the tier explicitly in audit output.

After identifying thin instruction file content, produce a concise enrichment brief: what specific topics are undercovered, what domain principles the cache recommends addressing, and what concrete questions the instruction file should help the AI answer for this codebase. Do not just flag thinness — specify the gap so the implementer can act without additional research.

Community cache domain entries are pre-researched baselines, not mandates for every project. The audit applies them as candidate gaps, not requirements. A project may deliberately omit certain domain principles because they do not apply to its specific use case and architecture. Always verify whether a missing principle is actually relevant to this codebase before recommending it.
